phases.name: The Hook
phases.description: Introduce the scenario and the @Monster threat to the hunters.

phases.name: Negotiate Pay
phases.description: Test @Focus. You bargain for high pay, otherwise you agree to low pay. High pay is enough to have spending money later, low pay is enough only for subsistence.

phases.name: The Rumor Phase
phases.description: Meet someone who can tell you information about the @Monster. They present rumors for the @Monster (curated by the GM with true and misleading information). The GM chooses which of these rumors is true in this given scenario.

phases.name: The Research Phase
phases.description: An old journal, a tale from long ago, an old friend. You reach out to learn information about the @Monster, and test @Fate to learn one of its weakness.

phases.name: Days of Preparation
phases.description: Over a number of days (determined by the GM), hunters interact with NPCs, locations, and the surrounding environment to earn @Preparation Resources. Each interaction presents a skill challenge; on success the hunter earns a typed resource (@Alchemy, @Witchery, or @Smithery). Three resources of a single type unlock a powerful boon for the coming hunt.

phases.name: Tracking the Monster
phases.description: Find the @Monster in its home (test @Fate) or it ambushes you.

phases.name: Denouement
phases.description: Collect the bounty. If low pay, resupply up to 5 low-tier items from the Hunter's Arsenal. If high pay, each hunter may also acquire 1 high-tier item (including @Trick Weapons). @Nights Rest and @Leveling Up.

sections.title: Days of Preparation
sections.description: Before the hunt begins, hunters have a number of days (set by the GM) to prepare. Each day, the GM presents opportunities — NPCs, locations, and events — that hunters can interact with to earn @Preparation Resources. Resources carry between hunts and are spent to unlock powerful boons from three crafting trees: @Alchemy, @Witchery, and @Smithery.

subsections.title: How Preparation Works
rules: The GM decides how many days of preparation the hunters have before the hunt begins.
Each day, the GM determines what interactions are available — townspeople, shopkeepers, herbalists, blacksmiths, ruins, forests, or anything the scenario calls for.
Each interaction presents a skill challenge. The GM chooses the relevant stat (@Body, @Agility, @Focus, or @Fate) based on the nature of the interaction.
On a successful roll, the hunter earns one @Preparation Resource of the type associated with that interaction: @Alchemy, @Witchery, or @Smithery.
On a failed roll, the hunter gains nothing from that interaction.
@Preparation Resources persist between hunts. Hunters track their own resources independently but may choose to share resources with fellow hunters.

subsections.title: Resource Types
subsections.description: Each @Preparation Resource belongs to one of three types, corresponding to the three crafting trees. The type is determined by the nature of the interaction — the GM assigns the type when designing the scenario.
rules: @Alchemy — Earned from herbalists, apothecaries, alchemical labs, rare flora, and similar interactions.
@Witchery — Earned from mystics, haunted sites, seers, cursed objects, and similar interactions.
@Smithery — Earned from blacksmiths, armorers, mines, forges, and similar interactions.

subsections.title: Boons
subsections.description: When a hunter collects 3 resources of a single type, they may spend them to unlock a boon. Each tree offers a different advantage for the coming hunt.

subsections.title: Alchemy — Adapt the Hunter
subsections.description: The hunter brews a powerful elixir that heightens their reflexes and senses.
rules: Cost: 3 @Alchemy resources.
Effect: The hunter rolls an additional d10 on all rolls — including rolls with @Advantage or @Disadvantage.
Duration: The first @Adapt the Hunter boon lasts 24 hours. It is not permanent.

subsections.title: Witchery — Curse the Hunted
subsections.description: The hunter places a supernatural curse upon the target monster.
rules: Cost: 3 @Witchery resources.
Effect: The @Monster takes 1 additional @Damage per hit when it takes damage, while in the presence of this hunter.
Duration: The first @Curse the Hunted boon is permanent for this monster.

subsections.title: Smithery — Forge the Weapon
subsections.description: The hunter adapts a single weapon to be devastatingly effective against the target monster.
rules: Cost: 3 @Smithery resources.
Effect: The hunter chooses one weapon. That weapon now deals 3 @Damage per hit to the @Monster it was adapted to kill.
Duration: The first @Forge the Weapon boon is permanent for this weapon against this monster.

subsections.title: Stacking Boons
subsections.description: A hunter may attempt to stack a second boon of the same type by spending 3 more resources (6 total in one tree). This doubles the effect but carries serious risk. A third stack is fatal.

subsections.title: Second Boon Effects
rules: A second @Adapt the Hunter grants a second additional d10 on all rolls (two extra total). Lasts 24 hours.
A second @Curse the Hunted causes the @Monster to take 2 additional @Damage per hit in this hunter's presence. Lasts 24 hours.
A second @Forge the Weapon further empowers the adapted weapon, now dealing 6 @Damage per hit to the adapted @Monster. Lasts 24 hours.

subsections.title: Second Boon Risk
subsections.description: Stacking a second boon is dangerous. The hunter must pass a risk roll or suffer a severe consequence.
rules: @Alchemy risk: Roll 1d10 against @Body. On failure, the hunter's blood is poisoned — they gain the @Diseased condition.
@Witchery risk: Roll 1d10 against @Fate. On failure, the hunter's fate is cursed — their @Hit Points are reduced to 1.
@Smithery risk: Roll 1d10 against @Focus or @Agility (hunter's choice). On failure, the weapon breaks — it becomes useless and must be replaced hastily.

subsections.title: Second Boon Duration
rules: All second-stack boons last 24 hours, regardless of the tree.
The first boon's permanence (for @Witchery and @Smithery) is unaffected — only the second layer expires.

subsections.title: Third Boon — Death
subsections.description: Attempting to stack a third boon of the same type is always fatal. There is no roll.
rules: @Alchemy: The toxins in the hunter's blood reach lethal concentration. The hunter dies.
@Witchery: The weight of cursed fate stops the hunter's heart. The hunter dies.
@Smithery: The overcharged weapon detonates in the hunter's hands. The hunter dies.

sections.title: Hunter's Arsenal
sections.description: Hunters carry specialized equipment for the hunt. All equipment falls into one of four subcategories — @Weapon, @Protection, @Control, or @Denial — and is divided between two tiers: @Low Pay and @High Pay. A hunter may carry up to 5 items at a time.

subsections.title: Equipment Rules
rules: A hunter may carry up to 5 items at a time.
At character creation, choose 1 low-tier weapon and 4 additional low-tier items of your choice.
After a hunt with low pay: resupply up to 5 low-tier items.
After a hunt with high pay: each hunter may acquire 1 high-tier item (including @Trick Weapons), in addition to resupplying low-tier items.

subsections.title: Weapons
subsections.description: Arms designed to kill monsters. All weapons deal 1 damage on a successful attack (2 on a @Critical Hit). High-tier weapons are called @Trick Weapons — each has a hidden mechanism or enchantment that grants a bonus effect.
rules: All weapons deal 1 @Damage (2 on a critical).
Weapons specify a @Damage type (@Physical Damage, @Spiritual Damage, or @Hybrid Damage) and a range (@Close, @Near, or @Far).
@Trick Weapons are high-tier weapons with a special bonus effect described on the item.

subsections.title: Protection
subsections.description: Armor, shields, wards, and relics that guard the hunter. Protection items specify which damage types they resist and are required to @Brace.
rules: A hunter must have a Protection item to use the @Brace combat action.
Protection items specify which damage type(s) they guard against: @Physical Damage, @Spiritual Damage, or @Hybrid Damage.
High-tier Protection items may guard against all damage types or grant additional defensive effects.

subsections.title: Control
subsections.description: Implements that bind, stall, move, or manipulate monsters — forcing them into unfavorable positions.
rules: Control items restrict a monster's movement, actions, or positioning.
Many Control items are consumable with limited uses per hunt.
Some Control items require a stat test to deploy successfully.

subsections.title: Denial
subsections.description: Items that strip monsters of their advantages — preventing abilities, forcing @Wind Up, or shutting down attacks.
rules: Denial items suppress, counter, or remove monster advantages.
Many Denial items are consumable with limited uses per hunt.
Denial items can neutralize @Special Abilities, buffs, and immunities.

subsections.title: Low Pay Tier
subsections.description: Standard hunter equipment available at character creation and during resupply after any hunt.

subsections.title: High Pay Tier
subsections.description: Rare and powerful equipment — including @Trick Weapons. Acquired only after hunts with high pay. Each hunter may acquire 1 high-tier item during the @Denouement.

subsections.title: Explanation of Hidden Creatures
rules: Monsters have a rating of @Insight required before a hunter can see them as they are. These creatures are metaphysical threats that challenge perception of the physical realm.
All monsters that lie beyond a human's @Insight appear as beasts rationalized by the context the human has. They might perceive a werewolf as a large bear, or a changeling as a deformed man.

subsections.title: The Downsides of Insight
subsections.description: Having higher @Insight enables hunters to perceive entities that others cannot. However, exceeding your fellow hunters' @Insight scores may pose risks. If only your hunter has the required @Insight to see a creature, then only your hunter can @Attack it effectively, but this also draws the creature's attention who will target your hunter viciously. Fellow hunters, lacking the required @Insight, would struggle to assist effectively.

subsections.title: Reading Monster Tables
subsections.description: Monster actions are determined by rolling a d10 and comparing to the @Attack value.
subsections.mechanics: Roll 1d10. If the result is equal to or less than the monster's @Attack value, the monster @Attack actions. If higher, the monster '@Wind Up' for a more deadly following @Attack.
example_actions: @Wind Up: Failed @Attack roll means next @Attack deals double @Damage
Special Ability: Some monsters have unique powers
Multi-attack: Some monsters can target multiple creatures

subsections.title: Monster Stats Explained
subsections.description: Understanding how to read and use monster statistics during play.

stat_explanations.description: The number or lower that must be rolled on a d10 for the monster to attack. Higher rolls result in 'Wind Up'.
How much damage the monster deals when it successfully attacks. This number is doubled if the monster 'Wound Up' the previous turn.
Read as (number of players × multiplier) + addition. Example: '0+1' = (players × 0) + 1 = 1 HP. '5' with 3 players = 15 HP.
Multiply this number by the number of players. When the monster's HP falls below this threshold, it will attempt to flee or negotiate to save its life.
Special modifiers that make the monster more resilient or dangerous.
What happens when the monster's attack roll is a natural 1. Follow these instructions instead of applying normal damage.
Damage types the monster is immune to (if any).
Stat values rolled when the monster needs to make ability checks.
The insight level required to see the monster as it truly is, rather than as some sort of guise.
What people see when they have an insight score below the monster's Insight value. Monsters with Insight 0 have no guise and appear as they truly are.
Unique vulnerabilities that can be discovered during the research phase of a hunt.

subsections.title: Damage and Wind Up
subsections.description: Monsters deal their @Damage value when they successfully @Attack. The '@Wind Up' mechanic makes failed @Attack actions more dangerous.
subsections.mechanics: When a monster fails to @Attack (rolls higher than @Attack value), it '@Wind Up'. The next successful @Attack deals double the normal @Damage value.

subsections.title: Examples of Special Abilities
examples: @Dragons Breath deals @Damage equal to the @Monster's @Hit Points
@Special Abilities triggered on a 1 (maybe a @Breath Weapon, something that does 3 @Damage)
Other unique abilities specific to each @Monster

subsections.title: Hit Points and Bloodied Calculation
subsections.description: @Hit Points are calculated based on the number of players, and @Bloodied thresholds determine when @Monster become desperate.
examples: @Bloodied '2' with 3 players: @Monster flees or negotiates when below 6 @Hit Points
